inputs.story: The Bennington Kennel Club is putting together a feature article on some of their past dog show winners. Match each dog to its breed and owner, and determine the year in which it won "Best in Show". Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
inputs.constraints:
1. The dalmatian won 1 year after Fernando's canine.
2. Ginger's dog won 1 year before the irish setter.
3. Ginger's canine is the bulldog.
4. The four dogs are the dog that won in 2006, the dalmatian, the bulldog and Elsie's dog.
inputs.constants:
owners: Anita; Elsie; Fernando; Ginger.
years: 2006; 2007; 2008; 2009.
breeds: bulldog; dalmatian; irish setter; maltese.
inputs.solution:
owners	years	breeds
ginger	2008	bulldog
anita	2007	dalmatian
elsie	2009	irish setter
fernando	2006	maltese

asp_generation.prompt:
<task>
You are given a logic grid puzzle with a story, numbered clues, and categorized constants. Your task is to generate a complete ASP (Answer Set Programming) program that, when solved with Clingo, yields a unique answer set representing the puzzle's solution.

Provided below are:
- two examples: `example_1` and `example_2`,
- the problem you must solve: `problem_to_solve`,
- important rules: `rules`.
</task>

<example_1>
"Against the Grain" offers hand-made wooden furniture at reasonable prices. Each item is made by an in-house employee. Using only the clues that follow, match each item to the employee who crafted it, and determine its price and the type of wood used to make it. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.

Constants:
Employees: Bonita; Yvette; Tabitha.
Prices: $225; $275; $325.
Wood types: ash; poplar; sandalwood.

ASP Program:
```asp
% Constants
employee("Bonita"; "Yvette"; "Tabitha").
price(225; 275; 325).
wood_type("ash"; "poplar"; "sandalwood").

% Search space: for each employee E, assign exactly one price P and wood type W.
{match(E, P, W): price(P), wood_type(W)}=1 :- employee(E).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{E1=E2; P1=P2; W1=W2}=0 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), (E1,P1,W1)!=(E2,P2,W2).

% 1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
P=325 :- match(E,P,W), E="Bonita".

% 2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
P1>P2 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), W1="poplar", E2="Yvette".

% 3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
P1=P2-50 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), E1="Tabitha", W2="sandalwood".

% 4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.
{W="ash"; E="Yvette"}=1 :- match(E,P,W), P=275.

#show match/3.
```
</example_1>

<example_2>
The Winter Olympics have just wrapped up in Norway. Using only the clues that follow, determine the number of gold and silver medals won by each country. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. The four teams are the team from Bolivia, the team that won 3 gold medals, the team that won 6 silver medals, and the team from Argentina.
2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
4. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams.
5. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
6. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.

Constants:
Countries: Argentina; Bolivia; Grenada; Oman.
Silver medals: 2; 6; 10; 11.
Gold medals: 1; 2; 3; 4.

ASP Program:
```asp
% Constants
country("Argentina"; "Bolivia"; "Grenada"; "Oman").
silver_medals(2; 6; 10; 11).
gold_medals(1; 2; 3; 4).

% Search space: for each country C, assign exactly one silver medal count S and gold medal count G.
{assign(C, S, G): silver_medals(S), gold_medals(G)}=1 :- country(C).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{C1=C2; S1=S2; G1=G2}=0 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), (C1,S1,G1)!=(C2,S2,G2).

% 1. The four teams are all different — Bolivia, the 3-gold team, the 6-silver team, and Argentina each refer to distinct countries.
{C="Bolivia"; G=3; S=6; C="Argentina"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G).

% 2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Oman", S2=10.

% 3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
{G=2; G=1}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), C="Oman".

% 4. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams — three pairwise inequalities.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C2!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C1!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".

% 5. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
{C="Argentina"; S=2}=0 :- assign(C,S,G), G=4.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Argentina", S2=2.

% 6. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.
{S=6; C="Grenada"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), G=2.

#show assign/3.
```
</example_2>

Here is the problem to be solved:
<problem_to_solve>
The Bennington Kennel Club is putting together a feature article on some of their past dog show winners. Match each dog to its breed and owner, and determine the year in which it won "Best in Show". Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. The dalmatian won 1 year after Fernando's canine.
2. Ginger's dog won 1 year before the irish setter.
3. Ginger's canine is the bulldog.
4. The four dogs are the dog that won in 2006, the dalmatian, the bulldog and Elsie's dog.

Constants:
owners: Anita; Elsie; Fernando; Ginger.
years: 2006; 2007; 2008; 2009.
breeds: bulldog; dalmatian; irish setter; maltese.
</problem_to_solve>

Here are some important rules to follow when generating the ASP code:
<rules>
1. Constant declarations — "category(const1; const2; ...)."
   - Category names: lowercase with underscores only (e.g., wood_type, gold_medals).
   - All constants in the same category must use the same format — all integers or all quoted strings.
   - Prefer integers: strip special characters ($225 → 225), strip units while keeping the number ("3 days" → 3, "2 months" → 2), and convert verbal or ordinal numerals (once → 1, twice → 2, third → 3, September → 9).
   - Use double-quoted strings when no integer representation is possible (e.g., "Bonita", "9:30AM").

2. Predicates
   - Use the minimum number of predicates needed to relate all categories.
   - Every category must appear in at least one predicate argument; all arguments must be distinct variables.
   - Choose a name that reflects the relationship (e.g., match, assign, schedule, launch).

3. Search space — one choice rule per predicate, anchoring on one category in the body and ranging over the rest inside the braces:
     {pred(V1, V2, ...): cat2(V2), cat3(V3), ...}=1 :- cat1(V1).

4. Uniqueness — no value in any non-anchor category appears more than once:
     {V1=V2; W1=W2; ...}=0 :- pred(V1,W1,...), pred(V2,W2,...), (V1,W1,...)!=(V2,W2,...).

5. Constraint rules — encode each clue using one or more rules of these forms, using only defined constants and predicates:
     <C1>; ...; <Cm> :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.      % if all Li hold, at least one Ci must hold
     {<C1>; ...; <Cm>}=k :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.  % if all Li hold, exactly k of Ci hold
     :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.                        % this combination is forbidden

   Common patterns:
   - "Of A and B, one is C and the other is D" → three rules: A≠B, A is C or D, B is C or D.
   - "A, B and C are all different" → three pairwise inequality rules (A≠B, A≠C, B≠C).
   - "Neither A nor B is X" → {A_cond; B_cond}=0 :- ... X ... .

6. Show directives — one "#show pred/arity." per predicate.
</rules>

ASP Program:
asp_generation.thinking:
The problem asks us to match each dog to its breed and owner, and determine the year it won "Best in Show".
Categories:
owners: Anita; Elsie; Fernando; Ginger.
years: 2006; 2007; 2008; 2009.
breeds: bulldog; dalmatian; irish setter; maltese.

There are 4 owners. Each owner has exactly one dog, which has a breed and a year.
Wait, the problem says "Match each dog to its breed and owner, and determine the year in which it won 'Best in Show'."
So we have owners: Anita, Elsie, Fernando, Ginger.
For each owner, we assign a breed and a year.
Let's define the predicate as `win(Owner, Year, Breed)`.

Constants:
owner("Anita"; "Elsie"; "Fernando"; "Ginger").
year(2006; 2007; 2008; 2009).
breed("bulldog"; "dalmatian"; "irish setter"; "maltese").

Search space:
{win(O, Y, B): year(Y), breed(B)}=1 :- owner(O).

Uniqueness:
{O1=O2; Y1=Y2; B1=B2}=0 :- win(O1,Y1,B1), win(O2,Y2,B2), (O1,Y1,B1)!=(O2,Y2,B2).

Clues:
1. The dalmatian won 1 year after Fernando's canine.
   -> If Fernando has year Y, dalmatian has year Y+1.
   So: Y_dalm = Y_fernando + 1.
   We can encode this as:
   Y1 = Y2 + 1 :- win("Fernando", Y2, B2), win(O1, Y1, "dalmatian").
   Wait, the format for constraints/rules in the prompt uses `:-` for constraints, but clues can also be implications. The prompt says:
   `<C1>; ...; <Cm> :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.`
   `:- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.`
   For equality/inequality, we can just use:
   `Y1 = Y2 + 1 :- win("Fernando", Y2, B2), win(O1, Y1, "dalmatian").`
   Or we can use `:- not (Y1 = Y2 + 1), win("Fernando", Y2, B2), win(O1, Y1, "dalmatian").` but the simpler form is fine in Clingo.

2. Ginger's dog won 1 year before the irish setter.
   -> Y_ginger + 1 = Y_irish.
   `Y1 = Y2 + 1 :- win("Ginger", Y1, B1), win(O2, Y2, "irish setter").`
   Wait, Ginger's dog is the bulldog (clue 3). So Ginger's year is Y_ginger. Irish setter's year is Y_irish.
   `Y1 = Y2 + 1 :- win("Ginger", Y1, B1), win(O2, Y2, "irish setter").`

3. Ginger's canine is the bulldog.
   `B="bulldog" :- win("Ginger", Y, B).`

4. The four dogs are the dog that won in 2006, the dalmatian, the bulldog and Elsie's dog.
   This implies these four refer to distinct entities.
   So:
   - Dog in 2006 != Dalmatian
   - Dog in 2006 != Bulldog
   - Dog in 2006 != Elsie's dog
   - Dalmatian != Bulldog
   - Dalmatian != Elsie's dog
   - Bulldog != Elsie's dog

   Let's encode pairwise inequalities:
   `:- win(E1, 2006, B1), win(E2, Y2, "dalmatian"), E1=E2, B1="dalmatian".` -> Actually, we want to say they are different owners.
   So:
   `E1!=E2 :- win(E1, 2006, B1), win(E2, Y2, "dalmatian").`
   `E1!=E3 :- win(E1, 2006, B1), win(E3, Y3, "bulldog").`
   `E1!="Elsie" :- win(E1, 2006, B1).`
   `"dalmatian"!="bulldog" :- win(E1, Y1, "dalmatian"), win(E2, Y2, "bulldog").` (Actually breeds are distinct by uniqueness constraint, but we can just state owner inequalities)
   Let's stick to owner inequalities:
   `O1!=O2 :- win(O1, 2006, B1), win(O2, Y2, "dalmatian").`
   `O1!=O3 :- win(O1, 2006, B1), win(O3, Y3, "bulldog").`
   `O1="Elsie" :- false. % O1!="Elsie" :- win(O1, 2006, B1).`
   `O2!=O3 :- win(O2, Y2, "dalmatian"), win(O3, Y3, "bulldog").`
   `O2!="Elsie" :- win(O2, Y2, "dalmatian").`
   `O3!="Elsie" :- win(O3, Y3, "bulldog").`

   Wait, clue 4 says: "The four dogs are the dog that won in 2006, the dalmatian, the bulldog and Elsie's dog."
   This means these four descriptions refer to four different dogs.
   So:
   2006 dog != dalmatian
   2006 dog != bulldog
   2006 dog != Elsie's dog
   dalmatian != bulldog
   dalmatian != Elsie's dog
   bulldog != Elsie's dog

   We can encode these as owner inequalities:
   `E1!=E2 :- win(E1, 2006, _), win(E2, _, "dalmatian").`
   `E1!=E3 :- win(E1, 2006, _), win(E3, _, "bulldog").`
   `E1!="Elsie" :- win(E1, 2006, _).`
   `E2!=E3 :- win(E2, _, "dalmatian"), win(E3, _, "bulldog").`
   `E2!="Elsie" :- win(E2, _, "dalmatian").`
   `E3!="Elsie" :- win(E3, _, "bulldog").`

   This covers all pairwise distinctness.

Let's verify the clues and constraints carefully.
Clue 1: `Y_dalm = Y_fernando + 1`
Clue 2: `Y_ginger + 1 = Y_irish` (since Ginger's dog won 1 year before Irish setter)
Clue 3: `B_ginger = "bulldog"`
Clue 4: 4 distinct entities.

Check years: 2006, 2007, 2008, 2009.
From clue 1: Fernando's year can be 2006, 2007, 2008. Dalmatian's year is +1.
From clue 2: Ginger's year can be 2006, 2007, 2008. Irish setter's year is +1.
From clue 3: Ginger has bulldog.
From clue 4: 2006 dog is not Elsie. So Elsie won in 2007, 2008, or 2009.
Also, 2006 dog is not dalmatian, not bulldog. So 2006 dog is maltese (since dalmatian and bulldog are taken, and Elsie is not 2006). Wait, could 2006 dog be irish setter? Yes.
But clue 4 says the four are distinct: 2006, dalmatian, bulldog, Elsie.
So 2006 != dalmatian, 2006 != bulldog, 2006 != Elsie.
Dalmatian != bulldog, Dalmatian != Elsie, Bulldog != Elsie.

Let's assign variables to owners: Anita, Elsie, Fernando, Ginger.
Breed of Ginger = bulldog.
So bulldog != Elsie -> Ginger != Elsie (obviously).
Dalmatian != Elsie -> Dalmatian owner is not Elsie.
2006 != Elsie -> Elsie won in 2007, 2008, or 2009.

Let's check consistency.
If Ginger = bulldog, and Dalmatian != Elsie, then Dalmatian owner is Anita or Fernando.
If 2006 != Elsie, 2006 != Dalmatian, 2006 != Bulldog (Ginger), so 2006 owner is Anita or Fernando.
Also, Dalmatian and 2006 are distinct owners. So one of them is Anita, the other is Fernando.
Thus, Elsie and Ginger are the remaining owners.
Ginger has bulldog.
Elsie must have irish setter or maltese.
But Dalmatian is Anita or Fernando.
2006 is the other of Anita/Fernando.
Ginger (bulldog) year?
Fernando year?
Dalmatian year = Fernando year + 1.
If Fernando = 2006, Dalmatian = 2007. Then 2006 is Fernando. But 2006 != Dalmatian (satisfied). 2006 != Bulldog (satisfied). 2006 != Elsie (satisfied). So Fernando could be 2006.
If Fernando = 2007, Dalmatian = 2008. Then 2006 is Anita. Anita != Elsie (satisfied), Anita != Dalmatian (satisfied, Dalmatian is Fernando? No, Dalmatian is owner of Dalmatian, here Dalmatian year is 2008, owner is not Fernando because Fernando is 2007. Wait, Dalmatian's owner is the one with year 2008. So if Fernando=2007, Dalmatian=2008, owner of Dalmatian is Anita. But we said 2006 is Anita. Contradiction. So Fernando cannot be 2007.)
If Fernando = 2008, Dalmatian = 2009. Then 2006 is Anita. Dalmatian owner is Anita? No, Dalmatian year 2009, owner is Elsie or Ginger? But Ginger is bulldog. So Elsie is Dalmatian. But clue 4 says Dalmatian != Elsie. Contradiction.
So Fernando must be 2006.
Thus:
Fernando = 2006.
Dalmatian = 2007. Owner of Dalmatian is Anita (since 2006 is Fernando, 2007 is Dalmatian, 2006 != Dalmatian, so Fernando != Dalmatian owner. Owner of Dalmatian is Anita because Elsie != Dalmatian, Ginger = bulldog).
So Anita = 2007, breed = dalmatian.
Ginger = bulldog.
Elsie = ?
Years left: 2008, 2009.
Clue 2: Ginger's dog won 1 year before irish setter.
So Ginger year + 1 = Irish setter year.
If Ginger = 2008, Irish setter = 2009. Then Elsie = 2009, breed = irish setter.
If Ginger = 2009, Irish setter = 2010 (not in list). So Ginger must be 2008.
Thus:
Ginger = 2008, breed = bulldog.
Elsie = 2009, breed = irish setter.
Remaining: Anita = 2007, dalmatian. Fernando = 2006, maltese.
Check clues:
1. Dalmatian (2007) won 1 year after Fernando (2006). Yes.
2. Ginger (2008) won 1 year before Irish setter (2009). Yes.
3. Ginger's canine is bulldog. Yes.
4. Four dogs: 2006 (Fernando), Dalmatian (Anita), Bulldog (Ginger), Elsie's dog (Elsie). All distinct. Yes.
Solution:
Fernando: 2006, maltese
Anita: 2007, dalmatian
Ginger: 2008, bulldog
Elsie: 2009, irish setter

Now, construct the ASP code carefully following the rules.
Constants:
owner("Anita"; "Elsie"; "Fernando"; "Ginger").
year(2006; 2007; 2008; 2009).
breed("bulldog"; "dalmatian"; "irish setter"; "maltese").

Predicate: `win(O, Y, B)`

Search space:
{win(O, Y, B): year(Y), breed(B)}=1 :- owner(O).

Uniqueness:
{O1=O2; Y1=Y2; B1=B2}=0 :- win(O1,Y1,B1), win(O2,Y2,B2), (O1,Y1,B1)!=(O2,Y2,B2).

Clues:
1. `Y1 = Y2 + 1 :- win("Fernando", Y2, B2), win(O1, Y1, "dalmatian").`
2. `Y1 = Y2 + 1 :- win("Ginger", Y1, B1), win(O2, Y2, "irish setter").`
   Wait, clue says "Ginger's dog won 1 year before the irish setter." -> Ginger year + 1 = Irish setter year. So `Y_irish = Y_ginger + 1`.
   Rule: `Y2 = Y1 + 1 :- win("Ginger", Y1, B1), win(O2, Y2, "irish setter").`
3. `B="bulldog" :- win("Ginger", Y, B).`
4. Distinctness:
   `O1!=O2 :- win(O1, 2006, B1), win(O2, Y2, "dalmatian").`
   `O1!=O3 :- win(O1, 2006, B1), win(O3, Y3, "bulldog").`
   `O1!="Elsie" :- win(O1, 2006, B1).`
   `O2!=O3 :- win(O2, Y2, "dalmatian"), win(O3, Y3, "bulldog").`
   `O2!="Elsie" :- win(O2, Y2, "dalmatian").`
   `O3!="Elsie" :- win(O3, Y3, "bulldog").`

`C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Oman", S2=10.`
It's fine to use `:- win(O1, 2006, _), win(O2, _, "dalmatian"), O1=O2.` as a constraint `:-` but the prompt says we can use `<C1>; ...; <Cm> :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.` or `:- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.`
I will stick to the implication form `Y1 = Y2 + 1 :- ...` which is valid in Clingo.
Actually, to be safe with the "constraint rules" format in the prompt:
`:- win("Fernando", Y2, B2), win(O1, Y1, "dalmatian"), not (Y1 = Y2 + 1).` is safer if the solver doesn't like `Y1 = Y2 + 1 :- ...`
But Clingo handles `Y1 = Y2 + 1 :- ...` perfectly. I'll use it.
Alternatively, I can use `:- not (Y1 = Y2 + 1), win("Fernando", Y2, B2), win(O1, Y1, "dalmatian").`
I'll stick to the simpler `Y1 = Y2 + 1 :- ...` as in example 1: `P1>P2 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), W1="poplar", E2="Yvette".`
So `Y1 = Y2 + 1 :- win("Fernando", Y2, B2), win(O1, Y1, "dalmatian").` is fine.

Check clue 2: "Ginger's dog won 1 year before the irish setter." -> Ginger year + 1 = Irish setter year.
`Y2 = Y1 + 1 :- win("Ginger", Y1, B1), win(O2, Y2, "irish setter").`

Check clue 4 again: "The four dogs are the dog that won in 2006, the dalmatian, the bulldog and Elsie's dog."
This means these four refer to distinct dogs.
We can encode as:
`O1!=O2 :- win(O1, 2006, B1), win(O2, Y2, "dalmatian").`
`O1!=O3 :- win(O1, 2006, B1), win(O3, Y3, "bulldog").`
`O1!="Elsie" :- win(O1, 2006, B1).`
`O2!=O3 :- win(O2, Y2, "dalmatian"), win(O3, Y3, "bulldog").`
`O2!="Elsie" :- win(O2, Y2, "dalmatian").`
`O3!="Elsie" :- win(O3, Y3, "bulldog").`

This covers all 6 pairwise inequalities.

Wait, should I use `#show win/3.` at the end? Yes.

Let's double check the format of constants.
owners: Anita; Elsie; Fernando; Ginger. -> `owner("Anita"; "Elsie"; "Fernando"; "Ginger").`
years: 2006; 2007; 2008; 2009. -> `year(2006; 2007; 2008; 2009).`
breeds: bulldog; dalmatian; irish setter; maltese. -> `breed("bulldog"; "dalmatian"; "irish setter"; "maltese").`

Check rule 2: "Use the minimum number of predicates needed to relate all categories." -> `win/3` is fine.
Check rule 3: Search space `{win(O, Y, B): year(Y), breed(B)}=1 :- owner(O).`
Check rule 4: Uniqueness `{O1=O2; Y1=Y2; B1=B2}=0 :- win(O1,Y1,B1), win(O2,Y2,B2), (O1,Y1,B1)!=(O2,Y2,B2).`
Check rule 5: Clues encoded correctly.

One minor point: Clue 1 says "The dalmatian won 1 year after Fernando's canine."
Fernando's canine is his dog. So Fernando's year + 1 = Dalmatian's year.
`Y_dalm = Y_fernando + 1`.
My rule: `Y1 = Y2 + 1 :- win("Fernando", Y2, B2), win(O1, Y1, "dalmatian").` Correct.

Clue 2: "Ginger's dog won 1 year before the irish setter."
Ginger's year + 1 = Irish setter's year.
`Y_irish = Y_ginger + 1`.
My rule: `Y2 = Y1 + 1 :- win("Ginger", Y1, B1), win(O2, Y2, "irish setter").` Correct.

Clue 3: "Ginger's canine is the bulldog."
`B="bulldog" :- win("Ginger", Y, B).` Correct.

Clue 4: "The four dogs are the dog that won in 2006, the dalmatian, the bulldog and Elsie's dog."
Implies pairwise distinctness. My 6 rules cover it. Correct.

All looks solid.

Let's assemble the ASP program.
```asp
% Constants
owner("Anita"; "Elsie"; "Fernando"; "Ginger").
year(2006; 2007; 2008; 2009).
breed("bulldog"; "dalmatian"; "irish setter"; "maltese").

% Search space: for each owner O, assign exactly one year Y and breed B.
{win(O, Y, B): year(Y), breed(B)}=1 :- owner(O).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{O1=O2; Y1=Y2; B1=B2}=0 :- win(O1,Y1,B1), win(O2,Y2,B2), (O1,Y1,B1)!=(O2,Y2,B2).

% 1. The dalmatian won 1 year after Fernando's canine.
Y1 = Y2 + 1 :- win("Fernando", Y2, B2), win(O1, Y1, "dalmatian").

% 2. Ginger's dog won 1 year before the irish setter.
Y2 = Y1 + 1 :- win("Ginger", Y1, B1), win(O2, Y2, "irish setter").

% 3. Ginger's canine is the bulldog.
B="bulldog" :- win("Ginger", Y, B).

% 4. The four dogs are the dog that won in 2006, the dalmatian, the bulldog and Elsie's dog.
O1!=O2 :- win(O1, 2006, B1), win(O2, Y2, "dalmatian").
O1!=O3 :- win(O1, 2006, B1), win(O3, Y3, "bulldog").
```

```asp
year(2006; 2007; 2008; 2009).
breed("bulldog"; "dalmatian"; "irish setter"; "maltese").

O3!="Elsie" :- win(O3, Y3, "bulldog").

#show win/3.
```

```asp
% Constants
owner("Anita"; "Elsie"; "Fernando"; "Ginger").
year(2006; 2007; 2008; 2009).
breed("bulldog"; "dalmatian"; "irish setter"; "maltese").

% Search space: for each owner O, assign exactly one year Y and breed B.
{win(O, Y, B): year(Y), breed(B)}=1 :- owner(O).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{O1=O2; Y1=Y2; B1=B2}=0 :- win(O1,Y1,B1), win(O2,Y2,B2), (O1,Y1,B1)!=(O2,Y2,B2).

% 1. The dalmatian won 1 year after Fernando's canine.
Y1 = Y2 + 1 :- win("Fernando", Y2, B2), win(O1, Y1, "dalmatian").

% 2. Ginger's dog won 1 year before the irish setter.
Y2 = Y1 + 1 :- win("Ginger", Y1, B1), win(O2, Y2, "irish setter").

% 3. Ginger's canine is the bulldog.
B="bulldog" :- win("Ginger", Y, B).

% 4. The four dogs are the dog that won in 2006, the dalmatian, the bulldog and Elsie's dog.
O1!=O2 :- win(O1, 2006, B1), win(O2, Y2, "dalmatian").
O1!=O3 :- win(O1, 2006, B1), win(O3, Y3, "bulldog").
O1!="Elsie" :- win(O1, 2006, B1).
O2!=O3 :- win(O2, Y2, "dalmatian"), win(O3, Y3, "bulldog").
O2!="Elsie" :- win(O2, Y2, "dalmatian").
O3!="Elsie" :- win(O3, Y3, "bulldog").

#show win/3.
```
